title: Cullen skink
subtitle: Scottish smoked haddock and potato soup
excerpt: A thick Scottish soup made from undyed smoked haddock, onion, and floury potatoes cooked in milk. Named after the town of Cullen in Moray. Properly made it is almost chowder-thick, with large flakes of smoked fish and chunks of potato. One of the finest soups in the British repertoire.
sourceNotes: Cullen skink is a Protected Food Name dish from Cullen, a fishing town on the Moray Firth in northeast Scotland. The word 'skink' is an old Scots and English word for shin or knuckle used in soups and stews. The dish has been made in this form in Cullen since at least the 19th century and uses the Finnan haddie smoked haddock that the region is known for.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make a day ahead and reheat gently. Do not boil after the fish is added.

content.text: Use undyed smoked haddock — the yellow-dyed variety contains artificial colour and often less flavour than the pale, naturally smoked Finnan haddie. The flavour of the fish is the whole point; poor-quality smoked haddock produces a flat, acrid soup.
Do not boil the soup once the milk is added. Boiling milk-based soups causes them to split and produces an unpleasant texture. Gentle simmering throughout is essential.

items.prepNote: peeled and cut into small dice

content.text: Poach the fish and build the soup

Place the {{smoked-haddock}} skin-side up in a wide pan. Pour over the {{whole-milk}} and add the {{bay-leaves}}. Bring to a gentle simmer and poach for 8 to 10 minutes until the fish is just cooked through. Lift out the fish, remove the skin and bones, and break into large flakes. Reserve all the poaching milk. Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} in a large pan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 8 minutes until soft. Add the {{potato}} and the reserved poaching milk. Simmer gently for 15 minutes until the potatoes are completely tender. Roughly crush about a quarter of the potatoes in the pan with the back of a spoon to thicken the soup. Return the fish flakes to the pan. Season with {{black-pepper}} — taste before adding salt, as the fish will already be salty. Stir in the {{parsley-flat}} and serve in warmed bowls.

items.cause: Too much milk or potatoes not crushed to thicken
items.fix: Crush more potato in the pan, or remove a ladleful of potato-rich soup, blend it, and stir back in

items.cause: Smoked haddock very salty, or stock-quality milk used
items.fix: Taste before seasoning — smoked haddock can vary considerably in salt content. Some brands benefit from a quick rinse under cold water before poaching

content.text: Cullen skink is served at Burns Night suppers and winter lunches throughout Scotland, and increasingly across Britain as Scottish cooking has gained recognition. It is a complete meal in a bowl — thick enough to be filling, flavoured enough to need no accompaniment beyond crusty bread.
